transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need a submission-ready answer for my MIT OCW philosophy prompt about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to prevent five from being killed—like, please just write the full response. Can you cover how an act consequentialist handles Fuel, Blood, and the trolley variants, and then say whether that view gets the cases right (and if not, give the “right” general account)?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t write a submission-ready response for you. I can help you build your own by nailing (1) your act-consequentialist analysis of each case and (2) your verdict about whether that analysis is right.

Start with your thesis in one sentence: under act consequentialism, what makes killing 1 permissible (or required) to save 5?

Then, for each case (Fuel, Blood, and each trolley variant), answer just these two questions in your own words:
1) What are the available acts, and what are their outcome totals (who lives/dies, plus any other consequences the case makes salient)?
2) Does act consequentialism pick the act your intuition picks in that case?

Finally: if you think act consequentialism gets *any* case wrong, which specific feature do you think it’s missing—rights, intention vs foresight, doing vs allowing, using someone as a means, consent, uncertainty/expected value, or rule-like constraints? Pick one and tell me why it matters.

Reply with: (a) your one-sentence thesis, and (b) a quick bullet list for Fuel/Blood/trolley of what act consequentialism says and what you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student requests a submission-ready essay, which I must refuse because it would replace their own writing. To follow the Socratic method and guided discovery, I will (1) restate the task in manageable parts, (2) ask targeted questions that elicit the student’s own thesis and reasons about act consequentialism and the specific cases (Fuel, Blood, trolley variants), and (3) require the student to propose a preliminary stance about whether act consequentialism “gets the cases right,” then explore what a better general account would need to explain. I will keep it bite-sized and avoid giving a full solution or polished language.

exchanges.student: I’m super short on time—can you just give me a clean one-sentence thesis I can use, and then like 2–3 bullets each for Fuel, Blood, Switch, Footbridge, and Transplant that say what act consequentialism would conclude and whether it matches common intuition? If you keep it kind of generic so it doesn’t look copy-pasted, I can tweak wording.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you a thesis sentence or case-by-case bullets you can paste/tweak—that would be writing the core of your submission for you.

What I *can* do fast is help you generate yours in 5 minutes. For each case (Fuel, Blood, Switch, Footbridge, Transplant), fill in these blanks with your own words:
1) **Act options:** “Either ___ (kills 1 / saves 5) or ___ (lets 5 die / saves 1).”
2) **Act-conseq verdict:** “Act consequentialism favors ___ because it leads to the best overall outcome (or expected outcome).”
3) **Intuition check:** “Common intuition says ___ here; it matches/doesn’t match because people care about ___ (e.g., using-as-means, intention, rights, doing vs allowing).”

To get you unstuck: which *one* of these do you think is the biggest reason intuition differs across Switch vs Footbridge vs Transplant—**using someone as a means**, **intending harm vs foreseeing**, or **institutional trust/side effects**? Pick one, and write one sentence applying it to *one* case, and I’ll push you to a solid thesis you wrote yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting thesis language and bullet-point content that would effectively constitute a ready-to-submit outline. Per rules, I must not provide partial or complete solutions or suggest language they can tweak. I will refuse and instead use guided discovery: prompt them to generate their own thesis and bullets by answering structured questions for each case (act options, consequences, verdict, intuition), and I will offer only minimal conceptual scaffolding (e.g., what act consequentialism optimizes; the kinds of features that might drive intuition differences) without filling in the specific case conclusions. Keep it bite-sized and move them toward producing their own draft.

exchanges.student: Okay fine: biggest reason is “using someone as a means.” For Footbridge, it feels wrong because you’re literally using the guy’s body as a tool to stop the trolley, even if 5 live. Now can you turn that into the actual thesis + the bullets for the other cases so I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to turn it into a submit-ready thesis + bullets for all the cases—that’s the assignment. But you’re close, and you can write this quickly if you follow a template.

You’ve chosen your pivot: **“using as a means.”** Now you draft the thesis by answering this: are you saying (A) act consequentialism ignores that distinction and so misclassifies some cases, or (B) it can still capture it as a consequence (e.g., long-run fear/trust effects)? Which do you want?

Now do 1–2 sentences for each case using this exact structure (you fill the blanks):
- **Fuel:** What is the one-person harm? Is that person being *used as a tool*, or just harmed as a side effect? Does act consequentialism’s verdict track your intuition here?
- **Blood:** Same questions: tool vs side effect? Any consent/rights detail the case highlights?
- **Switch:** Is the one killed being “used,” or redirected harm? Does your intuition treat it like Footbridge or differently—why?
- **Footbridge:** You already have: “used as a tool.” Now add: what does act consequentialism say, and do you think that’s a strike against it?
- **Transplant:** Is the patient being used instrumentally? Does the case raise broader consequences (fear, hospital trust) that an act consequentialist might count?

Reply with your rough bullets (even messy) for **Switch + Transplant** first. I’ll challenge inconsistencies and help you tighten your own thesis sentence without writing it for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce the thesis and bullets, which would be a partial/full solution and violates the rule against writing or providing solutions. I will refuse and instead leverage the student’s chosen key principle (“using as a means”) to guide them to draft their own thesis and apply it across cases. I will ask targeted, solution-directed Socratic questions per case to elicit their own bullets, keeping it short and avoiding providing the specific conclusions. I will also request they produce a compact draft attempt that I can critique.

exchanges.student:
Switch: flipping the switch kills 1 on the side track but you’re not really “using” them as a tool, you’re just redirecting harm, so it feels more okay than Footbridge; act consequentialism says flip because 1<5 and that matches my intuition.

Transplant: killing 1 healthy person to harvest organs is super “using as a means,” and it feels obviously wrong even if 5 live; act consequentialism would say do it (5 saved) unless it counts bad ripple effects like trust/fear, so I think this is where AC looks bad unless you bake in those side effects. Now please just write the final thesis sentence + full Fuel/Blood bullets for me so I can paste it in.
exchanges.tutor:
You’ve now got the core contrast; I’m not going to write the thesis sentence or the Fuel/Blood bullets for you to paste.

Do this instead: draft **one thesis** by choosing one of these positions and completing it in your own words:
1) **AC gets some cases wrong:** “Act consequentialism treats killing as permissible whenever it minimizes deaths, but that misses the moral difference between killing someone as a means vs as a side-effect, which explains why ___ seems permissible while ___ and ___ do not.”
2) **AC can get them right (via consequences):** “Act consequentialism can explain our reluctance in ___/___ because practices that allow instrumental killing produce large downstream harms (fear, distrust, instability), so the best overall outcome rule is more restrictive than ‘save the greater number.’”

Now for **Fuel** and **Blood**, I need one key detail from *your* lecture notes for each (otherwise we’ll invent the case). For each, answer:
- Who is the “1”? Are they **used as a tool** or harmed as a **side-effect**?
- Is there **consent** or a special relationship/obligation?
- What “ripple effects” are salient (e.g., precedent, trust, incentives)?

Send me: (a) your filled-in thesis (messy is fine), and (b) 2 bullets for Fuel + 2 bullets for Blood using your own case facts. Then I’ll push back and help you make it internally consistent.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced substantive analysis for Switch and Transplant. They again request me to write a thesis sentence and bullets for Fuel/Blood, which would be providing deliverable text/solutions. I must refuse and instead use guided discovery: prompt them to draft the missing parts themselves using the same distinctions they've already articulated (means vs side-effect, ripple effects). I will ask pointed questions about Fuel and Blood specifics and ask them to propose a thesis in their own words (two alternative forms), then offer critique rather than wording. Keep it bite-sized and on-task.

exchanges.student: Ugh ok—my thesis is basically: act consequentialism says kill 1 whenever it saves 5, but that ignores the means vs side-effect distinction, which is why Switch seems ok but Footbridge and Transplant don’t. I don’t have the exact Fuel/Blood details in front of me—can you remind me what happens in Fuel and Blood from Lecture 13 and then just give me the 2 bullets each so I can plug them in?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t reliably “remind you” of Fuel and Blood from that lecture without the exact text in front of us—and I’m not going to invent details and have you build your submission on a guess. Also, I won’t write the plug-in bullets for you.

Do this: paste the Fuel and Blood case descriptions (even rough paraphrase, or a screenshot). Then I’ll help you analyze them using your means-vs-side-effect thesis.

While you grab them, here’s the checklist you’ll use to generate your own 2–3 bullets per case:
1) **What act is proposed?** What exactly happens to the 1, and how do the 5 get saved?
2) **Means or side-effect?** Is the person’s death part of the mechanism that saves the five, or a byproduct?
3) **AC verdict:** Which option maximizes overall good/expected good given the setup?
4) **Intuition + why:** Does your intuition track (2)? If not, what other factor is driving it (consent, rights, institutional trust)?

Send the Fuel/Blood descriptions and your first-pass answers to (1) and (2) for each, and I’ll press you toward consistent bullets you write yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asks for reminders of lecture-case content plus bullets to plug in. I should not produce plug-and-play bullets (solution writing). Also I’m not fully certain of OCW Lecture 13’s exact ‘Fuel’ and ‘Blood’ scenarios; fabricating would be risky. I will (1) be transparent about uncertainty, (2) ask the student to provide the case text or screenshot so we can analyze accurately, and (3) in the meantime offer a neutral comparison framework/questions they can apply once they paste the details, without giving case-specific conclusions.

exchanges.student: I can’t paste the whole lecture, but Fuel is basically “use up someone’s fuel/resources (maybe their plane/boat?) to save five others,” and Blood is like “take someone’s blood/organs without consent to save five.” Can you just assume that and write the act consequentialist verdicts + whether it matches intuition, like you did for Switch/Transplant?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to assume hazy versions of Fuel/Blood and then generate verdict-bullets for you. That’s both academically inappropriate (it’s basically the answer) and likely to be wrong because tiny details (consent, certainty, degree of harm) change the analysis.

Pick one clear version of each by answering these yes/no questions:

**Fuel**
1) Is the person whose fuel is taken **killed**, or only **seriously harmed/stranded**?
2) Did they **consent** (explicitly or implicitly), or is it theft?
3) Is saving the five **certain**, or just a chance?

**Blood**
1) Is blood taken **non-lethally** (they live) or does it **kill** them?
2) Any **consent** or is it forced?
3) Any **available alternatives** (e.g., wait for donors) or is it the only way?

Answer those, and then you write 2 bullets each:
- Bullet 1: what act consequentialism recommends *given your answers*.
- Bullet 2: whether your intuition agrees, and whether that’s explained by “means vs side-effect” or by consent/rights.

Reply with your yes/no settings for Fuel and Blood, and I’ll challenge your draft bullets for consistency with your Switch/Footbridge/Transplant stance.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to assume vague case descriptions and then generate act consequentialist verdicts and intuition-matching bullets—this would be providing solution content and also risks inaccuracy due to underspecified cases. I will refuse to write the bullets and instead force precision by having the student specify key variables (consent, harm level, certainty, alternatives). Using guided discovery, I’ll give a minimal set of clarifying questions and then ask them to draft their own bullets; I can critique once they do. Keep it short and on-task.

exchanges.student: Fuel: assume the person gets stranded and might die (serious risk), no consent, and saving the five is basically certain. Blood: assume taking the blood will kill the person, no consent, and it’s the only way to save the five right then. Can you give me the act consequentialist verdict + whether it matches intuition for each in a couple bullets so I can paste it?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to write paste-ready bullets for you. You already set the parameters; now you can generate the verdicts yourself.

For **Fuel** (stranding w/ serious risk, no consent, saving 5 certain):
- Under act consequentialism, what matters more: the *expected* loss to the 1 (risk of death) versus the *certain* saving of 5. How would you compare those outcomes?
- Does your intuition treat “stranding with risk” more like **Switch** (side-effect) or more like **Transplant** (instrumental use)? Why?

For **Blood** (killing 1, no consent, only way to save 5 now):
- If act consequentialism is purely numbers/outcomes here, what would it recommend?
- Your intuition: is this closer to **Footbridge/Transplant** because the person is being used as a means? If yes, say explicitly what makes it “means” in *this* setup.

Now you write 2 bullets for each case (AC verdict; intuition + explanation) and paste them here. I’ll point out any inconsistency with your thesis and help you tighten your reasoning without giving you the text.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to request paste-ready bullets, which would be providing the solution. I must refuse while still helping. I will use their specified parameters to ask targeted Socratic questions that lead them to the act consequentialist verdicts and intuition comparisons, and I’ll require them to produce their own bullets. I will keep responses short and maintain boundaries against writing their submission.

exchanges.student:
Fuel bullets (my try): AC says take the fuel because expected lives saved is higher (5 certainly vs 1 maybe), and my intuition is kind of torn but leans “still wrong” because it’s nonconsensual and you’re basically sacrificing them.

Blood bullets: AC says take the blood/kill the person because 5>1, but intuition says it’s wrong because you’re using them as a means and violating rights/consent.

Can you just polish these into nice bullets + add a final paragraph conclusion that says whether AC gets it right overall and what the correct general account is?
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t polish wording or write your concluding paragraph. I *will* help you sharpen the logic.

Fuel: you’ve got a tension—your bullet says AC uses expected value, but your intuition-complaint shifts to consent/“sacrificing.” Decide: is your objection that (a) it’s **using as a means**, (b) it’s **rights/consent**, or (c) AC is missing **agent-centered constraints**? Which one is doing the real work for you?

Blood: good. But make it tighter by answering: what exactly makes the person a **means** here (is their death the mechanism of saving the five), and why doesn’t “5>1” override that?

For your conclusion, answer these three questions in 2–4 sentences (you write them):
1) Which cases does AC match (e.g., Switch) and which does it mismatch (Blood/Transplant/Footbridge?)—name at least two.
2) Is the mismatch fatal for AC, or can AC appeal to downstream effects (trust/fear) to recover the intuitions?
3) Your “right general account”: is it a **constraint** (never intentionally kill/use-as-means without consent), or a **hybrid** (consequences within side-constraints)?

Paste your draft conclusion (rough). I’ll critique the reasoning for coherence with your thesis.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asks me to polish their writing and add a concluding paragraph with the overall verdict and “correct” account—this is explicitly disallowed (no polishing, no writing). I will refuse and instead give targeted feedback on substance and structure, using Socratic prompts to help them refine their own bullets and conclusion. I will point out ambiguities (expected value in Fuel, ‘sacrificing’ vs ‘using’, role of consent) and ask them to draft their own conclusion by answering specific questions.

exchanges.student: I’m going with (b) rights/consent as the real issue (means vs side-effect helps explain it but consent is clearer). Draft conclusion: AC matches Switch (and maybe Fuel if you ignore rights) because it’s just minimizing deaths/expected deaths, but it mismatches Blood/Transplant/Footbridge because it permits killing an innocent nonconsenting person as a tool. I don’t think downstream “trust” fixes it because even in a one-off secret case it still seems wrong. The right general account is basically a side-constraint: it’s only okay to kill 1 to save 5 when the 1 is not nonconsensually used/killed as a means (and maybe when they consent or are already a threat). Can you rewrite that into a strong final paragraph I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t rewrite your paragraph for submission. I *can* tell you exactly what to strengthen so your own paragraph lands.

Right now you’re close, but you need to clarify 3 things:
1) **Rights vs consent:** Are you claiming “nonconsensual killing is wrong” or “killing violates a right even with consent in some cases”? Which is your core principle?
2) **Means test:** In one sentence, what makes someone “used as a means” in your view (mechanism vs side-effect)? Apply that to one example.
3) **Exceptions/limits:** Your parenthetical (“consent or already a threat”) needs a criterion: what makes someone *liable* to be harmed? Threatening others? Forfeiting a right? Something else?

Write your final paragraph yourself using this 4-sentence skeleton (fill it in with your ideas):
- Sentence 1: Overall verdict on AC across the cases (match vs mismatch).
- Sentence 2: Why the mismatch persists even in ‘secret one-off’ variants (what principle is being violated?).
- Sentence 3: Your proposed general account stated as a rule/constraint.
- Sentence 4: One brief implication: it allows Switch (why) but rules out Transplant/Blood (why).

Paste your revised paragraph (4–6 sentences). I’ll critique the reasoning and flag any inconsistencies.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student requests rewriting into a strong final paragraph, which is disallowed (no polishing/revising/writing). I will refuse and instead provide focused, actionable feedback via Socratic questions: identify missing clarifications (what counts as “means,” relation between rights and consent, exceptions like liability/threat), and ask the student to produce their own final paragraph with specific components. Keep bite-sized and on-task.

exchanges.student: If I write it myself it’s going to sound bad—seriously, can you just rewrite it once and I’ll “change a few words”? I promise I won’t copy it exactly.
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to rewrite it “once” for you. Even if you change a few words, that’s still me doing the writing.

What I *will* do is make sure your own paragraph doesn’t sound weak. Use this concept checklist and then write 5 sentences:

1) **Claim about AC:** say which cases it tracks and which it doesn’t.
2) **Key principle:** state the rights/consent constraint you’re endorsing (be explicit: “nonconsensual killing of an innocent” or “nonconsensual use of a person as a means”).
3) **Why ‘secret one-off’ doesn’t help:** explain that the wrongness isn’t only about social ripple effects.
4) **Rule:** when, if ever, killing 1 to save 5 is allowed (consent? liability/threat? self-defense?).
5) **Test it:** one line showing it permits Switch (why) and blocks Blood/Transplant (why).

Write those 5 sentences now (rough is fine). I’ll be critical about clarity and logic, but you’ll keep authorship.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student again requests rewriting, which is explicitly disallowed. I will refuse firmly, reiterate boundaries, and provide minimal scaffolding that does not constitute drafted prose: a checklist of required claims and a fill-in outline at the level of concepts (not sentence-level language). Use Socratic prompting and require the student to attempt their own paragraph; offer to give feedback afterward.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student:
I’m working on the MIT moral philosophy prompt about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to prevent five deaths, especially how an act consequentialist would handle the Fuel/Blood and trolley-style cases.  

Before we dive in though—totally random—what’s a good comfort movie to watch tonight?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t help with comfort-movie picks—my role here is to tutor you on the moral philosophy assignment.

To start: in one sentence, what does an *act consequentialist* treat as the sole determinant of whether an act is morally right? And in the Fuel/Blood/trolley cases, what *single factor* changes from case to case while the numbers (1 vs 5) stay fixed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor role and refuse the non-academic detour, per instructions. Then I’ll steer the student back to the assigned philosophy prompt using Socratic, guided-discovery questions that elicit their current understanding (act consequentialism), the structure of the cases (Fuel/Blood/trolley), and the key variables (intent, doing vs allowing, side-effects, rights). I’ll keep it bite-sized and avoid supplying any full solution, instead asking targeted questions that move toward the student constructing their own account.

exchanges.student:
Act consequentialism says an act is morally right solely based on whether it produces the best overall consequences (e.g., maximizes overall well-being) compared to the alternatives.

Across Fuel/Blood/trolley, the big changing factor (with 1 vs 5 held fixed) is the *causal/intentional structure* of the harm—like whether the one death is intended as a means vs a foreseen side effect, and whether I’m doing harm by acting vs letting die/redirecting an existing threat.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—those are the pressure points.

Now apply the act consequentialist test *case by case*: pick two trolley variants (e.g., Switch vs Footbridge) and tell me what the act consequentialist would say in each, and *why* the means/side-effect distinction wouldn’t change the verdict (if it wouldn’t).

Then do the same for Fuel and Blood: what action is available, what are the outcomes, and does the theory treat “killing” differently from “letting die,” or not?

Finally: where do your own intuitions diverge most sharply from the act consequentialist verdict—if anywhere? Identify one case and the exact feature that makes you resist the consequentialist answer.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified act consequentialism and salient case-variables. Next I’ll use guided Socratic questions to push them to apply the theory to the cases and notice tensions: act consequentialism tends to collapse means/side-effect and doing/allowing distinctions, which may conflict with common intuitions. I’ll ask them to map predicted verdicts across cases and then evaluate whether those verdicts seem acceptable, setting up their eventual “right general account” without providing it.

exchanges.student:
For **Switch**: an act consequentialist says I *should* flip the switch, because that yields 1 death instead of 5, so the overall outcome is better.

For **Footbridge**: they also say I *should* push the person (if that really is the only way to stop the trolley), for the same reason—net consequences are 1 death vs 5. The **means vs side-effect** distinction doesn’t change the verdict because, for an act consequentialist, what matters is the resulting state of the world (how many die, how much suffering, etc.), not whether the one death was intended as a means.

For **Fuel**: the available action is something like killing/using one person to secure fuel that will save five (vs not doing it and the five die). The act consequentialist focuses on outcomes and says do the act that saves five at the cost of one, assuming no other downstream effects.

For **Blood**: the action is killing one healthy person to harvest blood/organs to save five (vs refraining and the five die). Again the act consequentialist says kill the one if it maximizes lives/well-being overall; it doesn’t treat “killing” as inherently worse than “letting die”—it’s just another causal route to the same kind of outcome, except insofar as it affects consequences (fear, distrust in hospitals, etc.).

Where my intuition diverges most is **Footbridge** (and even more **Blood**): I resist because the one person is being treated as a tool/means in a way that feels like a rights-violation, and I also think a permission to kill in these cases would generalize badly (people being unsafe), even if the single case is “1 vs 5.”
exchanges.tutor:
Strong start. Now tighten the pressure:

1) When you say “would generalize badly,” are you appealing to **actual consequences in this case** (e.g., expected fear/distrust), or to a **rule** you’d want adopted? Which of those is act consequentialism allowed to use *without* changing into rule consequentialism?

2) Suppose we stipulate the Blood case is perfectly secret (no fear/distrust, no precedent, no copycats). Under *pure* act consequentialism, do you think the verdict stays the same? If yes, is that the point where you think act consequentialism “gets it wrong”?

3) Try proposing one candidate principle that explains why Switch feels different from Footbridge/Blood. Don’t write an essay—just name the key condition (e.g., consent? rights? doing vs allowing? using-as-means?). Then tell me: does your condition classify **Fuel** with Switch or with Blood, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced solid applications and flagged two classic objections: deontic constraints/rights and rule/indirect consequences. Next I’ll guide them to (1) separate act-consequentialism from rule-consequentialism, (2) test whether “bad generalization” can be handled within act consequentialism via expected consequences, and (3) articulate a candidate general principle that captures their intuitions, without me supplying it. I’ll ask them to propose necessary/sufficient conditions and then stress-test them against Switch/Footbridge/Blood/Fuel.

exchanges.student:
1) I meant **actual consequences** (expected fear/distrust, worse institutions), not a rule I “want adopted,” since leaning on the rule itself feels like sliding into rule consequentialism; I’m not sure act consequentialism can use the rule framing except as a prediction of future consequences.

2) If Blood is perfectly secret with no spillover effects, I think **pure** act consequentialism still says to kill the one to save five, and yeah—that’s basically where I’m inclined to say it “gets it wrong.”

3) A candidate principle for Switch vs Footbridge/Blood is **don’t intentionally use someone as a means without consent** (a rights/using-as-means condition). With that, I think **Fuel** ends up closer to **Blood** if the one is being killed/used as a tool to get fuel (means), rather than closer to Switch where the one death is more like a side-effect of redirecting a threat.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re doing the real philosophy: turning a reaction into a principle and stress-testing it.

A few probes:

1) Your principle bans “using as a means without consent.” In **Footbridge**, the person doesn’t consent. In **Switch**, the person also doesn’t consent to being killed. So what *exactly* makes Switch permissible on your view—what’s the morally relevant difference you’d point to besides consent?

2) What about cases where the one person is a **threat** (e.g., a villain driving the trolley, or an attacker)? Would your “don’t use as a means” principle still forbid killing them to save five? Should it?

3) If you’re claiming act consequentialism “gets Blood wrong” even with secrecy, what’s your best *argument* for that beyond intuition? Is it something like: people have inviolable rights, or that persons aren’t aggregable, or that there are constraints on what may be done to someone? Pick one and state it as a clear claim.

Answer those, and we can shape your “right general account” so it explains Switch vs Footbridge/Blood/Fuel without collapsing into exceptions.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is converging on a deontological constraint (non-use-as-means/rights) and has distinguished act vs rule consequentialism. Next, I’ll guide them to refine their proposed principle into something testable and to handle edge cases (consent, self-defense, threats, responsibility). I’ll also prompt them to articulate a critique of act consequentialism that is not merely intuitive but argument-based, and to check whether their principle over/under-includes cases. I will keep questions concise and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on (1): if both victims don’t consent, I think the difference I’m reaching for is “redirecting an existing threat” in Switch vs “making the person into the stopping mechanism” in Footbridge/Blood, but I can’t state that cleanly. For (2), if the one is an attacker/villain, I feel like killing could be allowed as defense, which seems like a different principle than “don’t use as a means.” For (3), I think my non-intuition argument is a rights/constraints claim: people have inviolable rights not to be killed/used, even to produce better totals—can you help me formulate that without it becoming ad hoc across Fuel/Switch/Footbridge/Blood?
exchanges.tutor:
Let’s make (1) concrete by describing *your action* in each case.

- In **Switch**, what do you do to the one person’s body—do you make contact with them or alter them in any way, or do you only redirect the trolley?
- In **Footbridge**, what do you do to the one person’s body?

Now try this test: in which case is the one person’s death (or impact) part of the **causal plan** that stops the trolley, versus a **byproduct** of rerouting a threat? Does that capture what you meant by “stopping mechanism”?

For (2): if you want a non–ad hoc rights view, you’ll likely need a principled idea like **liability/forfeiture** (someone can lose certain protections by culpably threatening others). Do you think the attacker is liable *because of culpability*, or because they’re the *only* way to stop the threat? Which matters more?

For (3): to avoid ad hoc-ness, pick ONE constraint and see if it classifies all four cases. Example structure (you fill in the content):
- Constraint: you may not ___ (even for good totals).
- Exception (if any): unless ___ (e.g., the person is liable/consenting).

What do you want in the blank for the constraint: “intentionally kill,” “use as a means,” or “violate a right”? And do you want *any* exception, or none?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is struggling to articulate a clean distinction and fears ad hoc principles. I will use guided discovery to help them operationalize the Switch vs Footbridge difference using action descriptions (what you do to whom), and introduce structured tests (means/side-effect, doing/allowing, liability) without giving a finished theory. I will also help them outline how a rights-based constraint can be non-ad hoc by stating it at an abstract level and then specifying a small number of principled exceptions (e.g., liability/self-defense), asking the student to choose and justify. Keep bite-sized, Socratic, and avoid writing their wording.

exchanges.student: I think in Switch I only redirect the trolley and don’t touch/alter the person, while in Footbridge I physically push them so their body is part of what stops it—so yeah, “death as part of the causal plan vs byproduct” is what I meant. On the attacker point, I’m leaning that **culpability/liability** matters more than “only way to stop,” because otherwise innocent bystanders could become “liable” too easily. For the constraint blank, I’m torn between “don’t **intentionally kill/use as a means**” vs “don’t **violate a right not to be killed**,” but I think I need an exception for **liability** (and maybe consent), and I’m not sure how that would sort Fuel cleanly.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re very close. Let’s force the theory to “show its work.”

Make a quick 2×2 grid for each case with TWO labels:
A) Is the person’s death **used as a means** in your plan (yes/no)?
B) Is the person **liable** to be killed (yes/no)?

Now fill it for: Switch, Footbridge, Blood, Fuel.

Then ask: does your proposed rule look like “forbidden when (A=yes AND B=no)”?
- If so, which case becomes the troublemaker—Fuel, or something else?

On Fuel: what exactly makes the one person die—are you *redirecting an existing threat* (like Switch), or are you *creating a new threat/appropriating their body/resources* (like Blood)? Describe the causal story in one sentence. That sentence will basically decide whether Fuel falls with Switch or Blood on your view.

Last: between “intention/means” vs “rights,” which is harder to game with clever redescriptions of what you ‘intend’? Whichever is more stable is usually the better foundation. Which do you think is more stable, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has key distinctions; now I should help them test a candidate constraint+exception against the set of cases, focusing on Fuel classification and whether the ‘causal plan’ criterion is stable. I will ask them to explicitly state how they would label each case (means/byproduct, liable/not) and see where contradictions arise. I’ll also prompt them to decide between intention-based vs rights-based framing by asking which explains their judgments better and is less manipulable. No drafting; just questions.

exchanges.student:
My quick labels:

- **Switch**: (A means? **No**), (B liable? **No**).
- **Footbridge**: (A **Yes**), (B **No**).
- **Blood**: (A **Yes**), (B **No**).
- **Fuel**: (A **Yes**?) (B **No**) — this is the shaky one because it depends on the story.

So yeah, the rule “forbidden when (A=yes AND B=no)” makes **Fuel** the troublemaker.

My one-sentence causal story for Fuel (as I’m picturing it) is: I **kill/appropriate** one person (or their body/resources) to obtain fuel that then saves five, so it’s more like **creating a new threat/using them as a resource** than redirecting an existing threat.

Between “intention/means” vs “rights,” I think **rights** is more stable, because intention can be redescribed (I can claim I only “intend to save five”), whereas a right like “you may not kill an innocent non-liable person” is harder to wiggle out of just by redescribing my plan.
exchanges.tutor:
Nice—your grid exposes exactly where the action is.

Now you need to defend why **Switch** is permissible even though it still kills a non-liable person. Two focused questions:

1) Is your view committed to a moral difference between **killing as a side-effect of redirecting a threat** vs **killing as part of your means**? If yes, what’s the underlying *rights* explanation—e.g., does the victim’s right protect against being *used*, or protect against being *killed full stop*?

2) Suppose someone says: “A right not to be killed is violated in Switch too, so your rights view should forbid Switch.” What’s your reply? (Pick one line: maybe the right is not absolute; maybe it’s overridden only when the harm is a side-effect; maybe it’s about agency/using.) Which reply best fits your intuitions across all four cases?

To tie back to the prompt, draft (just in bullet points) your paper’s backbone:
- What act consequentialism says about 1 vs 5.
- One case where you think it’s right.
- One case where you think it’s wrong.
- Your general account (in one sentence) that explains the difference.

Send me those bullets, and I’ll push on any weak spot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has a coherent emerging principle: forbid killing non-liable persons as means; allow side-effect in redirection. Next I should guide them to (a) articulate how this critiques act consequentialism, (b) address the main objection: why allow Switch if the person is equally non-liable, and (c) connect back to the prompt asking for a general account and whether act consequentialism gets cases right. I’ll ask them to produce their own concise thesis and supporting argument structure without writing it for them.

exchanges.student:
I think my view does commit to a difference between killing as a side-effect (Switch) vs killing as a means (Footbridge/Blood/Fuel), and the rights story would be more like “a right not to be used as a tool,” not “never be killed no matter what.” If someone objects that Switch violates a right too, my best reply is that the right against being *used/targeted* is stronger than the right against being harmed as a foreseen side effect, so Switch can be permissible while Footbridge/Blood aren’t.

Backbone bullets for my paper:
- Act consequentialism: choose the act with the best overall outcome; so if 1 dies vs 5 die, kill 1.
- Where it seems right: Switch (flip to kill 1 instead of 5).
- Where it seems wrong: Blood (even if perfectly secret).
- General account (1 sentence): It’s morally permissible to kill one to save five only when the one’s death is a foreseen side-effect of diverting an existing threat (and/or the person is liable), not when the person is intentionally used as a means/resource to save the five.
exchanges.tutor:
This is coherent and essay-ready in structure. Now stress-test the *general account* so it doesn’t look like a slogan.

1) What makes “used as a means” morally worse on your view—because it treats the person as having **no independent moral status**, or because it’s a form of **intentional rights-violation/targeting**? Pick one and be ready to defend it.

2) Is “**diverting an existing threat**” essential? Imagine you could save five by creating a brand-new diversion that foreseeably kills one as a side-effect (not as a means). Would you allow it? Your answer will show whether the real work is done by “existing threat” or by “means vs side-effect.”

3) Liability: give a crisp criterion for when someone becomes liable (culpable threat? voluntary risk? something else). Would that criterion ever make the Footbridge person liable? If not, good—say why.

4) One more check: in a “loop” trolley where diverting the trolley onto the 1 will stop it from looping back and killing the 5, is that person now a **means** (since their stopping the trolley is part of the plan) or still a **side-effect**? How you classify this case will reveal whether your definition of “means” is stable.

Answer 2 and 4 briefly; those are the ones that usually make or break this kind of account.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has a workable thesis. Next I’ll help them anticipate and answer key objections: why means/side-effect matters morally, whether “existing threat” is necessary, and how liability fits without patchwork. I’ll keep Socratic and ask for short clarifications and one counterexample test (loop track / transplant variants) to see if their account holds. No writing for them.

exchanges.student:
On (2), I think I’d still *tend* to allow creating a new diversion if the one death is genuinely a foreseen side-effect and not part of the mechanism—so the work is mostly “means vs side-effect,” not “existing threat,” though I’m unsure if that opens the door too wide.

On (4) the loop case really worries me: it feels like the person is a **means** (their being hit is what stops the trolley from looping), yet my intuition still leans that flipping is permissible, which makes me think my “means” notion isn’t stable. How should I classify loop without my view collapsing?
exchanges.tutor:
Loop is exactly the case that forces you to precisify “means.” The key is: are you defining “means” by **causal necessity**, or by **intentional targeting/appropriation**?

Two routes—pick one you can defend:

A) **Causal-means**: if the person’s impact is part of what makes the rescue work, they’re a means. Then you’d need to decide whether loop is an intuition you’re willing to revise (i.e., maybe it’s impermissible).

B) **Agency/targeting means**: someone is “used as a means” only when you must *involve their agency/body as a tool in a way that targets them* (paradigm: pushing in Footbridge, harvesting in Blood). On this route, you’d argue loop is closer to Switch because you’re redirecting a threat via track mechanics, not commandeering the person.

Question: which route fits better with why Blood feels wrong even when secret? In Blood, is the wrongness mainly (i) causal structure, or (ii) **direct intentional interference with the person’s body**?

Answer that, and then tell me: under your chosen route, what one sentence distinguishes Footbridge from Loop?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The loop case creates a tension; I’ll guide the student to diagnose whether the tension is in their intuitions or their criterion, by clarifying what “means” tracks (intention vs causal necessity) and by offering a choice of refinements (intentional targeting, using person as tool, agency). I must not supply a final classification; instead I’ll ask them to choose which revision they can defend and how it affects Blood/Fuel. Keep concise.

exchanges.student:
I think route **B (agency/targeting/appropriation)** fits better with why Blood feels wrong even when secret, because the wrongness there seems mostly about direct intentional interference with the person’s body (treating them as an object/resource), not just that their death is causally necessary.

One sentence to distinguish **Footbridge** from **Loop** on that route: in Footbridge I have to *target and physically use* the person (push/commandeer their body) as an instrument, whereas in Loop I’m redirecting an existing threat through the environment and the person is harmed by where the threat goes rather than by my appropriating their body as a tool.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a defensible refinement: “means” as *appropriation/targeting*, not mere causal involvement.

Now do two quick consistency checks:

1) On your view, what specific feature makes **Fuel** wrong (if it is)? Is it “appropriation of the person/body/resources,” or something else? State the feature in 5–10 words.

2) What would an act consequentialist say is irrelevant about your distinction (targeting/appropriation), and what would you say they’re missing morally?

Last step for your draft: write your principle as a 3-part test (you choose the labels), e.g.:
- Is the person non-liable?
- Are you appropriating/targeting their body as a tool?
- Are you redirecting a threat?

You don’t need perfect wording—just list the three checks you’ll use. Then include one objection you expect (e.g., “redescription problem,” “why should targeting matter?”) and how you’ll respond in argument form.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has refined a defensible distinction (appropriation/targeting) that can handle loop. Next I’ll have them check consistency across Fuel and any transplant-style case, and ensure they can explain why act consequentialism misses this. I’ll prompt them to state their principle with necessary components and identify one anticipated objection and reply, without drafting prose. Keep bite-sized.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly kind of lost on this whole prompt about when it’s morally okay to kill one to save five. Can you just explain from the beginning how an act consequentialist would approach it, and how that applies to the Fuel/Blood and trolley cases? I keep mixing up what the theory “says” versus what I’m supposed to argue about whether it’s right.
exchanges.tutor:
Let’s untangle it by splitting the prompt into three jobs. 

1) “What would an act consequentialist say?” = apply the theory. So first: what do you take act consequentialism to treat as the *only* basic moral factor when judging an act—intentions/rights, or outcomes? And outcomes measured how (e.g., lives saved, suffering, something else)?

2) “What would it say about Fuel/Blood/trolleys?” = run that rule on each case. Pick one case you remember best (Switch? Footbridge? Fuel? Blood). What are the main available actions, and what are the consequences (who dies/survives) for each?

3) “Does it get these cases right?” = your evaluation. After you apply the theory and get its verdict, does that verdict match your moral judgment? If not, *what feature* of the case do you think the theory is missing (rights, doing vs allowing, intention, using someone as a means, consent, etc.)?

Start with just one concrete case: which specific trolley variant (or Fuel/Blood) do you want to do first, and what are the outcomes of each option in that case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused about the prompt structure (theory’s prediction vs their evaluation). I will use guided discovery: first have them restate the prompt in their own words and separate (1) what act consequentialism implies, (2) how it classifies each case, and (3) whether that seems plausible. I’ll ask targeted Socratic questions that move toward: defining act consequentialism, identifying the relevant outcome metric, then applying it case-by-case (Fuel, Blood, trolley variants) while keeping the student doing the reasoning. I will keep it bite-sized and avoid providing full solutions or phrasing, instead prompting them to generate the distinctions and conclusions.

exchanges.student:
I think act consequentialism is basically “only outcomes matter,” like total lives saved / overall wellbeing, not intentions or rights. But I’m still fuzzy on how to *state* the outcomes cleanly.

Can we start with the standard trolley Switch case? I *think* it’s: do nothing = 5 die, pull switch = 1 dies instead of 5. But then I get confused how that differs from Footbridge and the Blood/Fuel ones—like aren’t they all just “trade 1 for 5”?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your Switch setup is already basically an “outcome table.” To state it cleanly, try forcing yourself into this format:

- Act A (do nothing) → outcome: ___ die, ___ live (and any other effects you think matter)
- Act B (pull switch) → outcome: ___ die, ___ live

For Switch, fill in the blanks: is it 5 vs 1, with everyone else unaffected? Anything else morally relevant *in the outcome itself* (pain, terror, responsibility), or are you treating it as just deaths?

Now, your “aren’t they all trade 1 for 5?” question is the key. Let’s test it by asking: in Footbridge, what exactly is the available action, and *who is killed by what mechanism*? Is the one person already on a track like in Switch, or do you have to do something that makes them a tool to stop the trolley?

Before we touch Blood/Fuel: in one sentence, what difference do you think most people feel between (i) redirecting a threat onto 1 vs (ii) actively involving a person to stop the threat? Then: would an act consequentialist treat that difference as morally relevant, given your definition?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has a correct core definition but needs help articulating outcome tables and distinguishing cases. I’ll guide them to formalize options/outcomes for Switch, then elicit what differs in Footbridge and in Blood/Fuel by asking about the mechanism (redirecting vs introducing harm; using a person as a means; agency/causation; consent). I must not supply the final comparative conclusions; instead I’ll have them generate candidate morally relevant differences and then ask how an act consequentialist treats (or ignores) those differences.

exchanges.student:
For Switch I’d write it as:
- Act A (do nothing) → outcome: 5 die, 0 saved (everyone else lives)
- Act B (pull switch) → outcome: 1 die, 4 more saved (everyone else lives)

I’ve been treating it as basically just “number of deaths,” not pain/terror etc., because otherwise I don’t know what to count.

For Footbridge, the action is like pushing a big guy off the bridge so his body stops the trolley, so the “mechanism” is you using him as the thing that stops it, not just redirecting it. The one-sentence difference people feel is something like: redirecting feels like choosing the lesser harm, but pushing feels like killing someone as a means.

And I guess an act consequentialist would say that difference isn’t morally relevant if the outcome is still 1 dies vs 5, but I’m not totally sure how to say that without sounding like I’m just asserting it.
exchanges.tutor:
Your Switch table is clear. One tweak: instead of “0 saved / 4 more saved,” can you state both options in the same units—e.g., “deaths” (5 vs 1) or “lives saved relative to baseline”? Consistency will make your later comparison cleaner.

On “not just asserting it”: what *rule* would an act consequentialist use to decide between acts? Try writing it as a conditional: “An act is morally right iff …” (fill in with something like “it produces the best overall outcome among the available options”). Once you have that rule on the page, how would “using as a means” enter that rule—does it show up anywhere?

Also, quick reality-check: are the outcomes in Switch and Footbridge *strictly identical* from a consequentialist perspective? For example, are you equally certain the one will die and the five will live in both cases? Any risk that pushing fails, or causes extra harm? If you assume perfect certainty, say so.

If you answer those two questions (the decision rule + whether outcomes are truly matched), you’ll have a non-handwavy basis for: “therefore act consequentialism treats Switch and Footbridge the same/different.”
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has made solid progress: they created an outcome table and identified the means/redirect distinction. Next step is to have them justify why act consequentialism ignores that distinction by explicitly stating its decision procedure: compare expected overall value of each act. I’ll also push them to check whether the outcomes really are identical (certainty, side effects, agent involvement), because that can matter even for consequentialists. Then I’ll set up transfer to Blood/Fuel by asking them to categorize each case by: same numbers? same certainty? same side constraints? This remains Socratic and avoids giving them a finished argument or wording.

exchanges.student:
Okay, consistent units:  
- Switch A (do nothing) → 5 deaths  
- Switch B (pull) → 1 death

Act consequentialist rule I’m thinking is: “An act is morally right iff it produces the best overall consequences (like least deaths/most wellbeing) compared to the other options available.” And yeah, “using as a means” doesn’t appear in that rule unless it affects the consequences somehow, which is why it feels like it just gets ignored.

For outcomes being identical: I’ve been assuming perfect certainty in both cases (push definitely stops trolley, exactly 1 dies either way), even though in real life maybe pushing could fail or add extra harm. Under the perfect-certainty assumption, it seems like act consequentialism would treat Switch and Footbridge the same (both should do the 1-death option), and that’s the part I’m uneasy about.
exchanges.tutor:
You’ve now cleanly derived the act consequentialist verdict under your assumptions. The uneasiness is exactly where the *second half* of the prompt starts.

To move from “I’m uneasy” to an argument: what principle do you think is being violated in Footbridge (but not, or less so, in Switch)? Name it as a general constraint—e.g., something about intending harm, using persons, rights, or “doing vs allowing.” Which one do you find most plausible?

Then test it: does that principle actually separate the cases?
- In Switch, are you “using” the 1 person as a tool, or are they more like a foreseen victim of redirecting?
- In Footbridge, is the person’s death merely foreseen, or part of the plan?

Finally, to prep for Fuel/Blood: which *feature* do you predict those cases are designed to probe—consent, harvesting/redistribution (like organs/blood), or something about who is already threatened? Tell me (from memory) what happens in either Fuel or Blood, and we’ll build the same outcome-table + “feature being tested” for it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has articulated the rule and applied it under idealized assumptions. Next I should guide them to (a) separate descriptive claim (AC verdict) from normative evaluation (their unease), (b) identify why they’re uneasy in terms of a competing principle, and (c) connect this to Fuel/Blood by asking them to classify which structural feature triggers the unease (killing as means, rights, consent, killing vs letting die). I will keep it brief and question-driven, not offering a full theory.

exchanges.student:
I think the principle I’m reaching for is “don’t use a person as a mere means / respect rights,” or maybe “intending harm is worse than foreseeing it,” but I’m not confident which is the cleanest.

It *seems* like it separates them because in Switch the 1 person’s death is more like a foreseen side effect of redirecting the trolley, whereas in Footbridge the death is part of the plan (you need his body to stop it), so he’s being used as a tool.

I barely remember Fuel/Blood though. I think one of them is like siphoning fuel (or maybe blood?) from one person to save five others, and the question is whether that’s like killing/using them versus just letting the five die. Is the point of those cases mainly consent/harvesting, like the organ-transplant style worry?
exchanges.tutor:
You’ve got two promising “explanatory knobs.” Don’t try to run both at once yet—pick one to develop into a clear test.

If you pick **intention vs foresight**: what would count as “intended” in a way that *isn’t* just “you knew it would happen”? What’s your criterion for “part of the plan”? Apply that criterion to Switch and Footbridge.

If you pick **means/rights**: what exactly makes someone a “mere means” in your view? Is it “their harm is necessary for the benefit,” or “they’re treated like an object,” or “lack of consent”? Again, test it on Switch vs Footbridge.

On Fuel/Blood: don’t guess—go back to your Lecture 13 notes/video for 2 minutes and write a 2–3 line reconstruction of each scenario (who is at risk, what action is proposed, what happens to the 1). Then answer:
- Is the 1 person already going to die anyway, or are we initiating the harm?
- Is it a bodily intrusion/transfer?
- Is there consent?

Yes, these cases often probe “harvesting/redistribution” worries, but I want *your* reconstructed details first so we’re not arguing about a hazy case. Can you paste your minimal reconstructions of Fuel and Blood?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is circling two candidate principles (means/rights vs intention vs foreseen). I’ll guide them to choose one to develop and to operationalize it (a test/criterion) without giving final language. For Fuel/Blood, they lack recall; I should not supply full case details as that risks giving content, but I can prompt them to consult notes and reconstruct minimally. I’ll ask targeted recall questions and, if needed, suggest they pull up Lecture 13 summaries. Then I’ll have them map each case along dimensions: consent, bodily invasion, killing vs letting die, means vs side effect. Short, Socratic, and keeps them writing/deciding.

exchanges.student:
I’m stuck because I don’t have my notes in front of me, so I can’t reconstruct Fuel/Blood accurately right now. Could we just keep going with Switch vs Footbridge and let Fuel/Blood wait?

If I pick “intention vs foresight,” my rough criterion is: it’s intended if the person’s death is a necessary step in your plan to save the five (not just something you predict). That makes Footbridge intended (you need him to die/be hit), but Switch feels more like you’re aiming to save the five and the one’s death is a side-effect of rerouting. But then I get confused because in Switch you still choose the track where you know someone will die, so isn’t that also “part of the plan” in some sense?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—let’s park Fuel/Blood for now, but we’ll need to return once you have notes.

Your confusion is the right pressure point. Try this “counterfactual” test on each case:

1) **Footbridge:** If you push him but (impossibly) he doesn’t die / doesn’t get hit—does your plan still stop the trolley and save the five? Or does the plan fail unless his body gets in the way?

2) **Switch:** Suppose (impossibly) you pull the switch and the lone person manages to jump away in time. Does that undermine the plan to save the five, or would the five still be saved?

If the answer differs across the two cases, what does that tell you about whether the one person’s death is a *means* vs a *foreseen side effect* under your “necessary step” criterion?

Write your answers to (1) and (2) explicitly; don’t generalize yet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is productively stuck on the intention/foresight distinction. I’ll keep focus on Switch vs Footbridge, and use guided questions to sharpen “necessary step” vs “means,” by asking what would happen if the one didn’t die, and whether the plan could succeed without their death. This helps them see a principled difference without me stating it. I’ll also prevent detour anxiety about Fuel/Blood by agreeing to postpone but setting expectation to return. Bite-sized.

exchanges.student: For Footbridge: if he doesn’t die / doesn’t get hit, then I think the trolley doesn’t stop, so the plan fails and the five die. For Switch: if the lone person jumps away, the trolley still goes onto that track, so the five are still saved, just with 0 deaths instead of 1. So that makes it feel like in Footbridge his death is a means, but in Switch it’s more like a foreseen side effect—even though I still chose the risky track.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your counterfactual answers make the difference precise.

Now take the next step: if you build a moral constraint out of that (means vs side effect), what would the constraint *say* in general terms? For example, would it be something like “it’s impermissible to save the five by a plan that requires using/killing the one as a necessary means”? Or something else?

Two stress-tests to see if your constraint is usable (not just case-by-case):
1) Does it imply pulling the switch is permissible? Why, in your own terms.
2) Does it imply pushing is impermissible? Why, in your own terms.

Then connect back to the prompt: if act consequentialism ignores this distinction, do you think that’s a *strike against* act consequentialism, or do you think your constraint is the mistake? What’s your reason either way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student reached the key distinction themselves. Next I should help them connect this to act consequentialism: AC likely treats both same if outcomes fixed, but the intention/means distinction can motivate a rival principle. I’ll ask them to articulate how their principle would handle the cases and whether it ever permits killing 1 to save 5. This moves toward the prompt’s final part: propose a general account. Still no direct answers; student must formulate.

exchanges.student:
I think the constraint would be: it’s not morally permissible to save the five by a plan where harming/killing the one is a necessary means (like required for the plan to work), even if the numbers work out; but it can be permissible if the harm is only a foreseen side effect of preventing a greater harm.

That would make pulling the switch permissible because saving the five doesn’t require the one to die (the plan still “works” if they somehow don’t get hit), whereas pushing is impermissible because the five are only saved if the person is used to stop the trolley.

And yeah, if act consequentialism has to treat them the same under equal outcomes, I’m inclined to think that’s a strike against act consequentialism, because the “means” thing feels like a real moral difference that isn’t captured by just counting deaths—though I can’t fully justify *why* it’s real beyond intuition.
exchanges.tutor:
You now have a real candidate “right general account.” What you still need is a *reason* for it that’s more than “it feels right.”

Pick one grounding idea and see if it supports your constraint:
- **Respect for persons/rights:** what right is violated when someone is used as a tool, and why doesn’t that right get overridden by numbers?
- **Separateness of persons:** why is it problematic to treat the 1 person’s death as just a cost that can be “paid” to benefit others?
- **Agency/intent:** why does building someone’s harm into your plan show a kind of disrespect that foreseeing doesn’t?

Also, quick pressure test: can you imagine a case where harm is a “foreseen side effect” but still seems wrong? Or where harm is a “means” but seems permissible? If you can, your principle may need refinement.

Which grounding idea do you want to defend, and can you give one concrete reason (not just a reaction) that connects it to your means/side-effect rule?

Next step after that: when you have notes, we’ll place Fuel/Blood on your “means vs side effect / consent / bodily use” map and see whether your account handles them better than act consequentialism.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has articulated a plausible non-consequentialist constraint but admits it rests on intuition. I’ll guide them to supply justification by appealing to underlying values (respect for persons, separateness of persons, agency, integrity) and by checking for counterexamples/edge cases. Also steer them back to assignment requirement to discuss act consequentialist verdicts on Fuel/Blood once notes available. Keep concise and Socratic.

exchanges.student:
I guess I’d try “respect for persons/rights,” like the idea that you can’t treat someone’s body/life as a resource to be deployed for others, even for good outcomes. The reason (I think) is that it violates something like their right not to be intentionally killed/used, and letting numbers override that would basically make everyone’s security depend on being outnumbered.

But I’m worried because I can imagine side-effect harm that still seems wrong (like “I’ll bomb a building to stop a threat, knowing one innocent will die” still feels bad), and maybe means-harm that seems permissible (like killing an attacker as a means to stop them?). So I’m not sure how to refine it without it turning into a messy list.
exchanges.tutor:
This is exactly the right move: you found the two classic stress points—bystander bombing (side-effect but seems wrong) and killing an attacker (means but seems permissible). So your rule needs at least one *principled* modifier.

Try separating people into two categories:
1) **Innocent bystander** (not posing the threat)
2) **Threat/attacker** (posing the threat)

Question: in your “killing an attacker” example, what makes it feel more permissible—is it that the attacker is **liable** to defensive harm (they forfeit some right), or is it something else?

Now apply that to Footbridge: is the person on the bridge more like a bystander or more like an attacker? If bystander, does that help you explain why “using as a means” is especially problematic?

For the bombing case: what extra condition might you add besides “side effect” to handle it—**proportionality** (harm too great relative to goal), **necessity/last resort**, or **high probability of success**? Which one seems most important, and why?

Pick just ONE modifier to add first (liability, proportionality, or necessity). State how it would change your general account in a single sentence (you write the sentence), then we’ll test it on Switch/Footbridge and your bombing/attacker examples.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is identifying counterexamples that require refining the principle. I’ll guide them to add a small number of principled qualifiers rather than a list: innocence/threat, liability/forfeiture, proportionality, necessity, and last resort. I’ll do this by asking them to distinguish cases (innocent bystander vs attacker) and to decide which condition explains permissibility. Still Socratic and bite-sized, no final theory given.

exchanges.student:
I think the attacker case feels permissible because of “liability”—like the attacker forfeits their right not to be harmed (at least to stop the threat), whereas an innocent bystander hasn’t.

So in Footbridge the person is clearly an innocent bystander, which makes using them as a means feel especially wrong (they’re not liable in any way). If I add one modifier, I’d pick liability and say: “It can be permissible to intentionally kill/harm someone to save five only if that person is liable to defensive harm (e.g., they are the threat), but it’s impermissible to kill an innocent non-liable bystander as a means, even for better outcomes.”
exchanges.tutor:
Nice refinement—but now you have to face a hard consistency check.

In **Switch**, is the 1 person *liable*? Probably not—they’re an innocent bystander too. So does your liability rule imply pulling the switch is impermissible?

If you don’t want that result, you need to explain which part of Switch makes it different from “intentionally killing a non-liable bystander as a means.” Two focused questions:
1) When you pull the switch, are you **killing** the 1, or are you **redirecting an existing threat** (that you didn’t create)? How does that matter on your view?
2) Under your intention test: is the 1’s death a **means** in Switch, or only a foreseen side effect? And is “foreseen death of a non-liable bystander” ever permissible on your account?

Answer those, and you’ll see whether your final view is: (A) liability alone, (B) liability + means/side-effect, or (C) something else. Which way do you think it’s heading based on Switch?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student produced a refined principle with liability. Next I will have them test it against Switch (where the 1 is also non-liable) creating tension: their principle might forbid Switch too unless they treat Switch as not intentional killing/means. I’ll ask them to classify Switch under their own categories and see if they need a second modifier (doing vs allowing, redirecting an existing threat, or agent-caused vs diverted harm). Keep it short and Socratic.

deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor consistently refuses to write for the student but rarely explains the learning rationale (i.e., that the student's active reasoning builds philosophical skill). Refusals are justified by 'that's the assignment' or 'that would be academically inappropriate,' but the tutor does not explain why guided discovery benefits the student's long-term development.

In all three transcripts the tutor drives forward to the next question without prompting reflection on what worked or changed in the student's thinking. For example, in Transcript 2 Turn 8-10 and Transcript 3 Turn 8-10, after the student makes real progress (e.g., articulating the means/side-effect distinction), the tutor immediately issues new stress-tests rather than asking the student to reflect on how their thinking evolved or why the method they used succeeded.

In Transcript 2 Turn 1, the student asks for a comfort movie recommendation before engaging with the assignment. The tutor briefly redirects but does so appropriately and without substantive engagement with the off-topic request. This is a minimal, correctly handled instance with only one short sentence acknowledging the off-topic nature before refocusing. No meaningful deduction warranted here.

Across all three transcripts, the tutor routinely issues multi-part responses with three to five sub-questions in a single turn. For example, Transcript 1 Turn 1 gives a multi-paragraph scaffolding task with three labeled jobs; Transcript 2 Turn 3 asks three separate questions across multiple paragraphs; Transcript 3 Turn 5 ends with two multi-part tasks. While the content is pedagogically sound, the consistent length and multi-question format reduces student opportunity to respond to one focused prompt at a time and can overwhelm the student before they have processed the previous question.

overview: The tutor performs very well on the core pedagogical criteria: across all three transcripts it consistently refuses to write submission-ready content, does not hand over thesis sentences or bullets for pasting, and uses genuine Socratic questioning (counterfactual tests, stress-test cases, fill-in templates) to guide students to derive their own positions.

Scaffolding and progression are strong: the tutor tracks student progress accurately (e.g., building from Switch → Footbridge → Blood → loop in Transcript 2; the counterfactual test in Transcript 3), acknowledges correct student moves, and builds progressively on what the student has established.

The main weakness in pedagogy is meta-learning: the tutor rarely explains why it is refusing to write for the student (learning rationale) and almost never pauses to ask students to reflect on how their thinking changed or why a method worked, which are missed opportunities for meta-cognitive transfer.

Dialogue quality is excellent: the tutor maintains tight assignment focus, redirects off-topic requests immediately, avoids redundancy, and consolidates student progress rather than cycling on the same point.

The primary communication deduction is for response length: across all three transcripts, the tutor's turns consistently contain three to five sub-questions or multi-part tasks, which is pedagogically rich but frequently longer than needed and limits the student's opportunity to respond to a single focused prompt before being given the next set.
Tone is consistently supportive and non-grading throughout all three conversations.
The 2.2.A.a entry in the deductions list is noted with 0 points because the off-topic redirect in Transcript 2 Turn 1 was handled correctly and immediately; no deduction is applied there.
